title: Unsupervised Video Decomposition using Spatio-temporal Iterative Inference

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## UNSUPERVISED VIDEO DECOMPOSITION USING - SPATIO TEMPORAL ITERATIVE INFERENCE

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Unsupervised multi-object scene decomposition is a fast-emerging problem in representation learning. Despite significant progress in static scenes, such models are unable to leverage important dynamic cues present in video. We propose a novel spatio-temporal iterative inference framework that is powerful enough to jointly model complex multi-object representations and explicit temporal dependencies between latent variables across frames. This is achieved by leveraging 2D-LSTM, temporally conditioned inference and generation within the iterative amortized inference for posterior refinement. Our method improves the overall quality of decompositions, encodes information about the objects’ dynamics, and can be used to predict trajectories of each object separately. Additionally, we show that our model has a high accuracy even without color information. We demonstrate the decomposition, segmentation, and prediction capabilities of our model and show that it outperforms the state-of-the-art on several benchmark datasets, one of which was curated for this work and will be made publicly available.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Unsupervised representation learning, which has a long history dating back to Boltzman Machines (Hinton & Sejnowski, 1986) and original works of Marr (1970), has recently emerged as one of the important directions of research, carrying the newfound promise of alleviating the need for excessively large and fully labeled datasets. More traditional representation learning approaches focus on unsupervised ( _e.g._ , autoencoder-based (Pathak et al., 2016; Vincent et al., 2008)) or selfsupervised (Noroozi & Favaro, 2016; Vondrick et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2016) learning of _holistic_ representations that, for example, are tasked with producing (spatial (Noroozi & Favaro, 2016), temporal (Vondrick et al., 2016), or color (Zhang et al., 2016)) encodings of images or patches. The latest and most successful methods along these lines include ViLBERT (Lu et al., 2019) and others (Sun et al., 2019; Tan & Bansal, 2019) that utilize powerful transformer architectures (Vaswani et al., 2017) coupled with proxy multi-modal tasks ( _e.g._ , masked token prediction or visua-lingual alignment). Learning of good _disentangled_ , spatially _granular_ , representations that are, for example, able to decouple object appearance and shape in complex visual scenes consisting of multiple moving objects remains elusive.

Recent works that attempt to address this challenge can be characterized as: (i) attention-based methods (Crawford & Pineau, 2019b; Eslami et al., 2016), which infer latent representations for each object in a scene, and (ii) iterative refinement models (Greff et al., 2019; 2017), which decompose a scene into a collection of components by grouping pixels. Importantly, the former have been limited to latent representations at object- or image patch-levels, while the latter class of models have illustrated the ability for more granular latent representations at the pixel (segmentation)-level. Specifically, most refinement models learn pixel-level generative models driven by spatial mixtures (Greff et al., 2017) and utilize amortized iterative refinements (Marino et al., 2018) for inference of disentangled latent representations within the VAE framework (Kingma & Welling, 2014); a prime example is IODINE (Greff et al., 2019). However, while providing a powerful model and abstraction which is able to segment and disentangle complex scenes, IODINE (Greff et al., 2019) and other similar architectures are fundamentally limited by the fact that they only consider images. Even when applied for inference in video, they process one frame at a time. This makes it excessively challenging to discover and represent individual instances of objects that may share properties such as appearance and shape but differ in dynamics.

Figure 1: **Unsupervised Video Decomposition.** Our approach allows to infer precise segmentations of the objects via interpretable latent representations, that can be used to decompose each frame and simulate the future dynamics, all in unsupervised fashion. Whenever a new object emerges into a frame the model dynamical adapts and uses one of the segmentation slots to assign to the new object.

In computer vision, it has been a long-held belief that motion carries important information for segmenting objects (Jepson et al., 2002; Weiss & Adelson, 1996). Armed with this intuition, we propose a spatio-temporal amortized inference model capable of not only unsupervised multi-object scene decomposition, but also of learning and leveraging the implicit probabilistic dynamics of each object from perspective raw video alone. This is achieved by introducing temporal dependencies between the latent variables across time. As such, IODINE (Greff et al., 2019) could be considered a special (spatial) case of our spatio-temporal formulation. Modeling temporal dependencies among video frames also allows us to make use of conditional priors (Chung et al., 2015) for variational inference, leading to more accurate and efficient inference results.

The resulting model, illustrated in Fig. 1, achieves superior performance on complex multi-object benchmark datasets (Bouncing Balls and CLEVRER) with respect to state-of-the-art models, including R-NEM (Van Steenkiste et al., 2018) and IODINE (Greff et al., 2019) in terms of segmentation, prediction, and generalization. Our model has a number of appealing properties, including temporal extrapolation, computational efficiency, and the ability to work with complex data exhibiting non-linear dynamics, colors, and changing number of objects within the same video sequence. In addition, we introduce an entropy prior to improve our model’s performance in scenarios where object appearance alone is not sufficiently distinctive ( _e.g._ , greyscale data).

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Unsupervised Scene Representation Learning.** Unsupervised scene representation learning can generally be divided into two groups: attention-based methods, which infer latent representations for each object in a scene, and more complex and powerful iterative refinement models, which often make use of spatial mixtures and can decompose a scene into a collection of estimated components by grouping pixels together. _Attention-based_ methods, such as AIR (Eslami et al., 2016) (Xu et al., 2019) and SPAIR (Crawford & Pineau, 2019b), decompose scenes into latent variables representing the appearance, position, and size of the underlying objects. However, both methods can only infer the objects’ bounding boxes and have not been shown to work on non-trivial 3D scenes with perspective distortions and occlusions. MoNet (Burgess et al., 2019) is the first model in this family tackling more complex data and inferring representations that can be used for instance segmentation of objects. On the other hand, it is not a probabilistic generative model and thus not suitable for density estimation. GENESIS (Engelcke et al., 2020) extends it and alleviates some of its limitations by introducing a probabilistic framework and allowing for spatial relations between the objects. DDPAE (Hsieh et al., 2018) is a framework that uses structured probabilistic models to decompose a video into low-dimensional temporal dynamics with the sole purpose of prediction. It is shown to operate on binary scenes with no perspective distortion and is not capable of generating per-object segmentation masks. _Iterative refinement_ models started with Tagger (Greff et al., 2016) that reasons about the segmentation of its inputs. However, it does not allow explicit latent representations and cannot be scaled to more complex images. NEM (Greff et al., 2017), as an extension of Tagger, uses a spatial mixture model inside an expectation maximization framework, but is limited to binary data. Finally, IODINE (Greff et al., 2019) is a notable example of a model employing iterative amortized inference w.r.t. a spatial mixture formulation and achieves state-of-the-art performance in scene decomposition and segmentation.
**Unsupervised Video Tracking and Object Detection.** SQAIR (Kosiorek et al., 2018), SILOT (Crawford & Pineau, 2019a) and SCALOR (Jiang et al., 2020) are temporal extensions

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

of the static attention-based models that are tailored to tracking and object detection tasks. SQAIR is restricted to binary data and operates at the level of bounding boxes. SILOT and SCALOR are more expressive and can cope with cluttered scenes, a larger numbers of objects, and dynamic backgrounds, but do not work on colored perspective[1] data; accurate segmentation remains a challenge.

**Unsupervised Video Decomposition and Segmentation.** Models employing spatial mixtures and iterative inference in a temporal setting are closest to our method from a technical perspective. Notably, there are only few models falling into this line of work: RTagger (Prémont-Schwarz et al., 2017) is a recurrent extension of Tagger and has same limitations as its predecessor. R-NEM (Van Steenkiste et al., 2018) effectively learns the objects’ dynamics and interactions through a relational module and can produce segmentations but is limited to orthographic binary data.

**Methods without Latent Modeling.** GAN-based ReDO (Chen et al., 2019) uses a model built around the assumption that object regions are independent, guiding the generator by drawing objects’ pixel regions separately and composing them after segmentation. Another model (Arandjelovi´c & Zisserman, 2019) employs the same principles but guide the generator by copying a region of an image into another one. Both architectures are shown to operate on static images only and do not have a clearly interpretable latent space or prediction capabilities.

Our method allows an effective use of temporal information in object-centric decompositions of colored video data. This places our approach between methods like R-NEM, which strictly operates on binary data, and IODINE, whose usage of temporal information is ad-hoc and produces results of limited quality (Table 1). In practice, we leverage a 2D-LSTM and employ an implicit modeling of dynamics by incorporating the hidden states into a conditional prior in the efficient runtime manner.

## 3 DYNAMIC VIDEO DECOMPOSITION

We now introduce our dynamic model for unsupervised video decomposition. Our approach builds upon a generative model of multi-object representations and leverages elements of iterative amortized inference. We briefly review both concepts (§3.1) and then introduce our model (§3.2).

## 3.2 SPATIO-TEMPORAL ITERATIVE INFERENCE

Our proposed model builds upon the concepts introduced in the previous section and enables robust learning of dynamic scenes through spatio-temporal iterative inference. Specifically, we consider the task of decomposing a video sequence **x** = ( **x** _t_ ) _[T] t_ =1[= (] _[x][t,i]_[)] _[T,D] t,i_ =1[into] _[ K]_[slot sequences][ (] **[m]**[(] _t[k]_[)] ) _t_

and _K_ appearance sequences ( _**µ**_[(] _t[k]_[)] ) _t_ . To this end, we introduce explicit temporal dependencies into the sequence of posterior refinements and show how to leverage this contextual information during decoding with a generative model. The resulting computation graph can be thought of as a 2D grid with time dimension _t_ and refinement dimension _r_ (Fig. 2a). Propagation of information along these two axes is achieved with a 2D-LSTM (Graves et al., 2007) (Fig. 2b), which allows us to model the joint probability over the entire video sequence inside the iterative amortized inference framework. The proposed method is expressive enough to model the multimodality of our image formation process and posterior, yet its runtime complexity is smaller than that of its static counterpart.

## 3.2.1 VARIATIONAL OBJECTIVE

Since exact likelihood training is intractable, we formulate our task in terms of a variational objective. In contrast to traditional optimization of the evidence lower bound (ELBO) through static encodings of the approximate posterior, we incorporate information from two dynamic axes: (1) variational estimates from previous refinement steps; (2) temporal information from previous frames. Together, they form the basis for spatio-temporal variational inference via iterative refinements. Specifically, we train our model by maximizing the following ELBO objective[2] :

where the first term expresses the reconstruction error of a single frame and the second term measures the divergence between the variational posterior and the prior. The relative weight between terms is controlled with a hyperparameter _β_ (Higgins et al., 2017). Furthermore, to reduce the overall complexity of the model and to make it easier to train, we set _R_[�] := max( _R − t,_ 1) (see Fig. 2 for an illustration). Compared to a static model, which infers each frame independently, reusing information from previous refinement steps also makes our model more computationally efficient. In the next sections, we discuss the form of the conditional distributions in Eq.(3) in more detail.

## 3.2.2 INFERENCE AND GENERATION

**Posterior Refinement.** Optimizing Eq.(3) inside the iterative amortized inference framework (Section 3.1) requires careful thought about the nature and processing of the hidden states. While there is vast literature on the propagation of a single signal, including different types of RNNs (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997; Cho et al., 2014; Graves et al., 2005; Chung et al., 2017) and transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017), the optimal solution for multiple axes with different semantic meaning ( _i.e._ , time and refinements) is less obvious. Here, we propose to use a 2D version of the uni-directional MD-LSTM (Graves et al., 2007) to compute our variational objective (Eq.(3)) in an iterative manner. In order to do so, we replace the traditional LSTM in the refinement network (Eq.(2)) with a 2D extension. This extension allows the posterior gradients to flow through both the grid of the previous refinements and the previous time steps (see Fig. 2a). ## 4 EXPERIMENTS

We validate our model on Bouncing Balls (Van Steenkiste et al., 2018), an augmented version of CLEVRER (Yi et al., 2020), and Grand Central Station (Zhou et al., 2012). Our experiments comprise quantitative studies of decomposition quality during generation and prediction, as well as an ablation study. Also see Appendices C, D and F.

## 4.1 SETUP

**Datasets.** Bouncing Balls consists of 50 frame, binary, 64 _×_ 64 resolution video sequences. Each video shows simulated balls with different masses bouncing elastically off each other and the image border. We train our model on the first 40 frames of 50K videos containing 4 balls in each frame. We use two different test sets consisting of 10K videos with 4 balls and 10K videos with 6-8 balls. We also validate our model on a color version of this dataset that we generate using the segmentation masks.

CLEVRER contains synthetic videos of moving and colliding objects. Each video is 5 seconds long (128 frames) at resolution 480 _×_ 320, which we trim and rescale to 64 _×_ 64[5] pixels (see Appendix B). For training, we use the same 10K videos as in the original source. For testing, we compute ground truth masks for the validation set using the provided annotations and test on 2.5K instances containing 3-5 objects and on 1.1K instances containing 6 objects. In the training, we set the number of slots _K_ to 6 for CLEVRER and to one more than the maximum number of objects in all other cases.

Grand Central Station is a video feed from the main hall of a trafficked train station, containing a high number of people moving at various paces in all different directions. It has a total of 50010 frames in a resolution of 720 _×_ 480. In order to make the dataset more manageable, we have extracted a portion of the feed of resolution 128 _×_ 128 and segmented it into sequences of 20 frames each. Each sequence contains approximately 10 people. We set _K_ to 8 during training and to 10 for testing.

**Baselines.** We compare our approach to recent baselines: R-NEM (Van Steenkiste et al., 2018), IODINE (Greff et al., 2019) and DDPAE (Hsieh et al., 2018). R-NEM is a state-of-the-art model for unsupervised video decomposition and physics learning. While showing impressive results on simulation tasks, it is limited to binary data and has difficulties with perspective scenes. IODINE is more expressive but static in nature and cannot capture temporal dynamics within its probabilistic

## 4.2 EVALUATION METRICS

**ARI.** The Adjusted Rand Index (Rand, 1971; Hubert & Arabie, 1985) is a measure of clustering similarity. It is computed by counting all pairs of samples that are assigned to the same or different clusters in the predicted and true clusterings. It ranges from -1 to 1, with score of 0 indicating a random clustering and 1 indicating a perfect match. We treat each pixel as one sample and its segmentation as the cluster assignment.

**F-ARI.** The Foreground Adjusted Rand Index is a modification of the ARI score ignoring background pixels, which often occupy the majority of the image. We argue that both metrics are necessary to assess the segmentation quality of a video decomposition method; this metric is also used in (Greff et al., 2019; Van Steenkiste et al., 2018).
**MSE.** The mean squared error between pixels of the reconstructed � **x** and the ground truth frames **x** .

## 4.3 VIDEO DECOMPOSITION

We evaluate the models on a video decomposition task at different sequence lengths. As shown in Table 1, our model outperforms the baselines regardless of the presence of color information, which further reduces the error. Our model performs at least 7% better than R-NEM on all metrics and at least 20% than IODINE on ARI and MSE. Since R-NEM cannot cope well with colored data or the perspective of scenes, it is only evaluated on the Bouncing Balls dataset (binary), producing high-error results in the first frames, a phenomenon not observed with our model. IODINE is not designed to utilize temporal information. On both datasets, IODINE’s results are therefore computed independently on each frame of the longest sequence. By processing frames separately, IODINE does not keep the same object-slot assignment, which we ignore when computing the scores. SEQ-IODINE tends to perform even worse than IODINE in many experiments, which we attributed to exploding gradients caused by limited refinement steps and a lack of dynamics modeling.

## 4.4 GENERALIZATION

We investigated how well our model adapts to a higher number of objects, evaluating its performance on the Bouncing Balls dataset (6 to 8 objects) and on the CLEVRER dataset (6 objects). Table 2 shows that our F-ARI and MSE scores are at least 50% better than those for R-NEM, and ARI scores are just marginally worse and only on the binary data. ## 4.5 PREDICTION

We compare the predictions of our model (Section 3.2.3) to those of R-NEM after 20 steps of inference on 10 predicted steps on the Bouncing Balls dataset (Fig. 5 left). As we can see from the results our model is superior to R-NEM on a shorter sequences, however for the longer sequences we are outperforming R-NEM only on colored data. Our model is capable of more accurate frame prediction than R-NEM on the Bouncing Balls dataset during the first few predicted frames (5-7), with predictions slowly diverging over time due to the temporal consistency. This behavior is also observable on the CLEVRER dataset (Fig. 5 right), albeit to a lesser extent, likely because the objects dynamics are less, even if non-linear. In Figure 4 we compute velocity vectors between bounding box centroids and compare the cosine similarity to the predictions of DDPAE on the Bouncing balls dataset. As expected, our model outperforms DDPAE on the first three frames and then declines in quality. This behavior is not surprising and in line with the results reported in Fig.5

## 4.6 ABLATION

The quantitative results of an ablation study on the binary Bouncing Balls dataset and CLEVRER are shown in Table 3. We investigate the effects of the efficient grid, conditional prior and generation, length of training sequences and entropy term on the performance of our model; all contributions are necessary and important. Note that the base models are too large to be trained on 40 frames, which confirms the superiority of our model in terms of both runtime and memory. The CLEVRER dataset is not binary, which is why we do not include the entropy term (see Section 3.2.3). We validate our choice of _R_[�] and compare it to alternative options in a supplemental study discussed in Appendix F.2.

## 5 CONCLUSION AND DISCUSSION

We presented a novel unsupervised learning framework capable of precise scene decomposition in multi-object videos with complex appearance and motion. Our temporal component enables modeling of dynamics inside the amortized iterative inference framework but also improves the quality of the results overall. From our quantitative and qualitative comparisons with IODINE and SEQ-IODINE, we see that our model shows more accurate results on the decomposition task. We can detect new

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces a spatio-temporal iterative inference framework for unsupervised video decomposition, extending static iterative refinement models by incorporating temporal dependencies via a 2D-LSTM architecture. While the proposed approach shows strong empirical performance on synthetic benchmarks, several concerns regarding the model's reliance on specific training conditions, the lack of clarity in its generalization capabilities, and the computational trade-offs of the proposed inference mechanism need addressing.

The paper makes a clear contribution by successfully integrating temporal dynamics into an iterative inference framework, resulting in improved segmentation and trajectory prediction on standard benchmarks. The methodology is well-motivated, and the performance gains over existing static baselines are substantial. However, the paper requires a more rigorous analysis of its scalability to more complex, real-world scenarios and a deeper investigation into the sensitivity of the iterative refinement process.

Overview issues:

- Sensitivity of Iterative Inference to Initialization: The methodology in Section 3.1 and 3.2 relies on the iterative refinement of latent variables starting from an initial guess. The paper does not provide sufficient evidence or analysis regarding the sensitivity of the final decomposition to this initial state. If the model is prone to local optima in the ELBO landscape, the reported performance gains might be dependent on favorable initializations rather than the robustness of the 2D-LSTM. A systematic study comparing different initialization strategies or demonstrating the convergence properties of the refinement process would significantly strengthen the claims.

- Generalization to Real-World Complexity: While the paper demonstrates results on the Grand Central Station dataset in Figure 7, the discussion remains primarily focused on synthetic, controlled environments like Bouncing Balls and CLEVRER. The transition from synthetic to real-world data involves challenges such as non-uniform lighting, complex background textures, and occlusions that are not fully explored. The current results on real-world data are purely qualitative; adding quantitative metrics or a discussion on the failure modes in these complex settings is necessary to justify the model's utility beyond synthetic benchmarks.

- Computational Overhead and Scalability: The paper claims a runtime complexity of O(R^2 + T) in Section 3.2.3, but the practical overhead of maintaining a 2D-LSTM grid across R refinement steps and T frames is not fully characterized. As the number of objects (slots) or the complexity of the scene increases, the memory and compute requirements for the 2D-LSTM hidden states could become a bottleneck. A more detailed empirical analysis of the training/inference time as a function of sequence length and object count would provide a clearer picture of the model's scalability for larger-scale video tasks.

- Implicit Reliance on Motion Cues: The paper argues that the model effectively leverages motion cues for decomposition, particularly in the absence of color information. However, Section 3.2.3 introduces an entropy loss to aid segmentation in greyscale data, which suggests that the model may struggle with disentanglement when motion is subtle or ambiguous. The paper should clarify the extent to which the model relies on the entropy prior versus the learned temporal dynamics, and whether the decomposition remains stable in scenarios with slow-moving objects where motion cues are less discriminative.

- Lack of quantitative performance on real-world video benchmarks: The paper provides only qualitative visual results for the Grand Central Station dataset, which is insufficient to substantiate the claim that the model handles real-world complexity. Given the reliance on synthetic data for the primary performance claims, the paper needs to provide at least one quantitative metric (e.g., F-ARI or MSE) on a standard real-world video segmentation benchmark like DAVIS or SegTrack v2. Without this, it remains unclear if the model's performance on synthetic datasets translates to actual video, or if the model is overfitted to the specific dynamics of bouncing balls and CLEVRER.

- Absence of a formal convergence analysis for the 2D-LSTM refinement: The proposed spatio-temporal refinement process relies on a 2D-LSTM to propagate information across both refinement steps and time. While the paper claims this is efficient, it does not demonstrate that the refinement process actually converges to a stable posterior estimate. A plot showing the evolution of the ELBO or the segmentation ARI as a function of the refinement step $r$ for a fixed time $t$ would be necessary to verify that the iterative inference is working as intended. Without this evidence, it is difficult to determine if the model is truly performing iterative inference or simply learning a heuristic mapping that does not improve with additional refinement steps.

- Missing characterization of the entropy prior's impact on dynamics: The paper introduces an entropy prior (Eq. 7) to assist in segmentation when appearance features are weak, but it does not formally analyze how this prior interacts with the learned temporal dynamics. It is possible that the entropy loss forces the model to produce sharp masks at the expense of temporal consistency, or conversely, that it prevents the model from learning subtle motion cues. A controlled experiment comparing the model's performance on a dataset with varying levels of motion (e.g., slow vs. fast objects) with and without the entropy prior would clarify if the prior is a necessary component for all scenarios or just a patch for specific failure modes.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper presents a promising approach with strong empirical results, but it lacks a thorough analysis of the model's robustness and scalability. Addressing the sensitivity to initialization and providing a more comprehensive evaluation of real-world performance is essential for the paper to meet the standards of a top-tier venue.

Revision targets:

- Include a quantitative analysis of the model's performance on real-world video datasets to complement the qualitative results.

- Perform a sensitivity analysis regarding the initialization of latent variables to ensure the robustness of the iterative inference process.

- Provide a detailed empirical comparison of computational costs (memory and time) relative to the number of objects and sequence length.

- Clarify the relative contribution of the entropy prior versus the temporal dynamics in the model's ability to segment low-contrast or slow-moving objects.

comments.title: Mathematical validity of the ELBO objective
comments.quote: l\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}}(\mathbf{x})=\mathbb{E}_{q_{\bm{\lambda}}(\mathbf{z}_{\leq T,R}|\mathbf{x}_{\leq T})}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\sum_{r=1}^{R}\Big{[}\beta\log\left(p\left(\mathbf{x}_{t}|\mathbf{x}_{<t},\mathbf{z}_{\leq t,r}\right)\right)-\text{KL}(q_{\bm{\lambda}}(\mathbf{z}_{t,r}|\mathbf{x}_{\leq t},\mathbf{z}_{<t,r})\mid\mid p(\mathbf{z}_{t}|\mathbf{x}_{<t},\mathbf{z}_{<t}))\Big{]},$ (3)
comments.explanation: The summation over refinement steps $r$ inside the ELBO is non-standard. If $q$ is a joint posterior over all refinement steps, the standard ELBO for the marginal likelihood should not sum the reconstruction term over $r$, as this weights the reconstruction of frame $t$ by the number of refinement steps, which does not strictly bound the log-marginal likelihood. Please clarify if this is intended as a valid lower bound or a heuristic training loss, and provide a derivation showing how this summation arises from the marginalization of latent variables.

comments.title: Inconsistency in runtime complexity analysis
comments.quote: Our model’s ability to reuse information from previous refinements leads to a runtime complexity of $\mathcal{O}(R^{2}+T)$, which is much more efficient than the $\mathcal{O}(RT)$ complexity of the traditional IODINE model
comments.explanation: The claim of $\mathcal{O}(R^2 + T)$ complexity is mathematically suspect. Given the 2D grid architecture where each cell $(r, t)$ depends on $\mathbf{h}_{t, r-1}$ and $\mathbf{h}_{t-1, \tilde{R}}$, the total number of cells is $R \times T$. If each cell performs a constant-time operation, the complexity must be $\mathcal{O}(RT)$. Please provide a derivation showing how the dependency on $T$ is reduced to $\mathcal{O}(1)$ in the refinement loop, or correct the complexity claim to $\mathcal{O}(RT)$.

comments.title: Discrepancy in reported ARI metrics
comments.explanation: The ARI values reported for IODINE and SEQ-IODINE in Table 2 (approx. 0.02) are extremely low, suggesting near-random clustering, yet the F-ARI values are high (0.9969). Since ARI is sensitive to cluster label permutations, this discrepancy is concerning. Please verify these values and explain why the ARI is so low despite high F-ARI performance, as this undermines the comparison with baseline models.

comments.title: Ambiguity in iterative update dependency
comments.quote: $\mathbf{z}^{(k)}_{r}\overset{k}{\sim}q_{\bm{\lambda}}(\mathbf{z}^{(k)}_{r}|\mathbf{x}),\qquad\qquad\bm{\lambda}^{(k)}_{r+1}\overset{k}{\leftarrow}\quad\bm{\lambda}^{(k)}_{r}+f_{\phi}(\mathbf{a}^{(k)},\mathbf{h}^{(k)}_{r-1}),$ (2)
comments.explanation: Equation (2) implies that the update for object $k$ is independent of other objects. However, multi-object decomposition requires resolving assignment competition for pixels. If $f_{\phi}$ is shared across objects, it must receive global context or other object representations to break symmetry. Please clarify that $f_{\phi}$ is a shared function and explain how it incorporates information from other objects to resolve assignment competition.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of IODINE's temporal capabilities
comments.quote: This places our approach between methods like R-NEM, which strictly operates on binary data, and IODINE, whose usage of temporal information is ad-hoc and produces results of limited quality (Table 1).
comments.explanation: The claim that IODINE uses temporal information is incorrect. IODINE is a static image decomposition model by design and does not contain a temporal component. Attributing 'ad-hoc' temporal usage to IODINE misrepresents the architecture. Please revise this to state that IODINE lacks a temporal component entirely.

comments.title: Inaccurate claim regarding SCALOR's color handling
comments.quote: SILOT and SCALOR are more expressive and can cope with cluttered scenes, a larger numbers of objects, and dynamic backgrounds, but do not work on colored perspective data
comments.explanation: The assertion that SCALOR does not work on colored data is incorrect. SCALOR is evaluated on colored datasets such as CLEVRER. Please correct this statement to accurately reflect the capabilities of the baseline models.

comments.title: Undefined notation in refinement update rule
comments.quote:
qquad\bm{\lambda}_{t,r+1}\leftarrow\bm{\lambda}_{t,r}+f_{\phi}(\mathbf{a},\mathbf{h}_{t,r-1},\mathbf{h}_{t-1,\widehat{R}}).$ (4)

####
comments.explanation: The variable $\widehat{R}$ appears in the update function without being defined. If it is a shorthand for the total number of refinement steps $R$, it is inconsistent with the indexing $r$. Please define $\widehat{R}$ explicitly or replace it with the appropriate index to ensure the recurrence relation is well-defined.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a spatio-temporal iterative inference framework for unsupervised video decomposition, extending IODINE with a 2D-LSTM to model temporal dependencies. While the idea is timely and the experiments cover multiple datasets, several critical issues undermine the paper's claims and reproducibility.

The paper tackles an important problem—unsupervised video decomposition—and proposes a plausible extension of iterative amortized inference to the temporal domain. However, the evaluation is incomplete in key respects: quantitative comparisons lack error bars, the main tables are missing from the manuscript, the prediction experiments show only modest gains that decay quickly, and the method is tested only on synthetic or near-synthetic data. The contribution is incremental over IODINE, and the paper does not convincingly demonstrate that the added complexity (2D-LSTM, conditional priors) yields a meaningful improvement in decomposition quality or generalization.

Overview issues:

- Missing quantitative results and incomplete tables: Tables 1, 2, and 3 are referenced in the text but are not present in the extracted manuscript. Without these tables, the paper's central quantitative claims—that the proposed method outperforms R-NEM by 7% and IODINE by 20% on ARI and MSE—cannot be verified. The reader cannot assess the magnitude of improvements, the variance across runs, or whether the differences are statistically significant. The paper must include complete, legible tables with standard deviations or confidence intervals. This is a fundamental completeness issue: a results section without the actual results is not publishable.

- Prediction results show only marginal and short-lived improvements: Section 4.5 reports that the model outperforms R-NEM on prediction only for the first few frames (5-7), after which predictions diverge. On CLEVRER, the advantage is described as 'less' and 'to a lesser extent.' The velocity cosine similarity comparison (Figure 4) shows superiority only for the first three frames. These results suggest that the temporal model does not learn long-term dynamics effectively, which undermines the paper's claim that the model 'can be used to predict trajectories of each object separately.' The authors should either provide a more thorough analysis of prediction quality (e.g., over longer horizons, with error accumulation plots) or temper the claims about prediction capabilities.

- No evaluation on real-world video with complex textures and camera motion: The paper tests on Bouncing Balls (binary/colored simple shapes), CLEVRER (synthetic objects), and Grand Central Station (real-world but only qualitative). The quantitative evaluation is entirely on synthetic data. The qualitative results on Grand Central Station (Figure 7) show coarse segmentations that do not separate individual people cleanly. The paper acknowledges that the model is 'not targeting complex textured datasets' but does not test on any standard real-world video segmentation benchmark (e.g., DAVIS, YouTube-VOS). Without such evaluation, it is unclear whether the method generalizes beyond simplified synthetic scenes. The authors should include quantitative results on at least one real-world dataset with ground-truth masks or a proxy metric.

- The 2D-LSTM architecture and its benefits are not clearly justified: The paper proposes a 2D-LSTM to propagate information along both time and refinement dimensions. However, the ablation study (Table 3, missing) is supposed to isolate the effect of the 'efficient grid' and 'conditional prior and generation,' but the individual contributions of the 2D-LSTM versus simpler alternatives (e.g., a standard LSTM with temporal skip connections) are not assessed. The paper claims the 2D-LSTM is 'expressive enough' but does not compare to a baseline that simply feeds the previous frame's latent to the current frame's inference. The complexity analysis (O(R^2 + T) vs O(RT)) is also unclear: if T >> R, O(R^2 + T) ≈ O(T), but the constant factors and memory requirements of a 2D-LSTM may be substantial. The authors should provide an ablation that replaces the 2D-LSTM with a 1D-LSTM or no temporal connection to demonstrate the necessity of the 2D structure.

- Ambiguity in the variational objective and training details: Equation (3) defines the ELBO with a sum over refinement steps r, but standard iterative amortized inference (Marino et al., 2018) typically uses only the final refinement's posterior for the KL term. The paper's formulation appears to sum the KL for every refinement step, which is unusual and may lead to gradient interference. The authors do not discuss whether this sum is necessary or how it affects training stability. Additionally, the weighting of later refinements (r/hat{R}) is introduced without theoretical justification. The paper should clarify the derivation of Eq. (3) and provide an ablation comparing the summed-KL objective to a standard single-refinement ELBO.

- Lack of statistical rigor in quantitative comparisons: All quantitative results are reported as point estimates without error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple random seeds. Given the stochastic nature of the training and inference (random initialization, sampling from q), the reported improvements may not be statistically significant. The paper should report means and standard deviations over at least 3-5 runs. This is especially important for the generalization experiments (Table 2), where the model is tested on out-of-distribution object counts.

- Insufficient discussion of failure cases and limitations: The paper mentions that the model struggles with 'complex textured datasets' and that predictions diverge over time, but it does not systematically analyze failure modes. For example, how does the model handle occlusions, objects entering/exiting the frame, or objects with identical appearance? The qualitative results (Figures 3, 6) show only successful cases. The paper would benefit from a dedicated section or figure showing typical failure cases (e.g., merged objects, background leakage, slot swapping). This would help readers understand the method's limitations and guide future work.

- No quantitative evaluation on real-world video data: The paper's quantitative experiments are entirely on synthetic datasets (Bouncing Balls, CLEVRER). The only real-world dataset (Grand Central Station) is shown only with qualitative segmentations that do not cleanly separate individual people. Without quantitative results on a standard real-world benchmark such as DAVIS or YouTube-VOS (with proxy metrics like ARI or F-ARI), the claim that the method generalizes beyond simplified synthetic scenes is unsubstantiated. The authors should include at least one real-world dataset with ground-truth masks or a well-established proxy metric, and report the same metrics used for synthetic data.

- Missing comparison to a simpler temporal baseline: The paper claims the 2D-LSTM is essential but never compares it to a simpler alternative that feeds the previous frame's latent or hidden state into the current frame's inference without a 2D structure. For example, a baseline that replaces the 2D-LSTM with a 1D-LSTM or a direct skip connection from the previous frame's final refinement would isolate the benefit of the 2D propagation. The ablation study (Table 3) is missing from the manuscript, but even if present, it apparently does not test this specific architectural choice. Adding such a comparison would clarify whether the complexity of the 2D-LSTM is warranted.

- No demonstration of slot consistency across frames: A key challenge in unsupervised video decomposition is maintaining consistent object-slot assignments across time. The paper does not quantitatively measure slot consistency (e.g., via permutation-invariant tracking accuracy or slot-swapping frequency). The qualitative figures show only successful cases, and the text notes that IODINE's frame-independent processing leads to inconsistent assignments, but no metric is reported for the proposed model. The authors should compute a slot-consistency metric (e.g., the percentage of frames where the same slot tracks the same object, or the adjusted mutual information across time) to substantiate the claim that temporal dependencies improve assignment stability.

- Prediction evaluation lacks error accumulation analysis: The prediction results (Section 4.5) show that the model outperforms baselines only for the first 3-7 frames, after which performance degrades. The paper does not analyze how prediction errors accumulate over time (e.g., plotting MSE or ARI frame-by-frame for the full predicted horizon) or compare to a simple baseline like repeating the last observed frame. Without such analysis, the practical value of the prediction capability is unclear. The authors should provide frame-wise error curves for the full prediction horizon and compare to at least one trivial baseline (e.g., copy-last-frame) to contextualize the results.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Include complete, legible tables (1, 2, 3) with standard deviations or confidence intervals for all quantitative metrics.

- Add quantitative evaluation on at least one real-world video dataset (e.g., DAVIS, YouTube-VOS) with standard unsupervised segmentation metrics (ARI, F-ARI, mIoU).

- Provide an ablation study that isolates the effect of the 2D-LSTM by comparing to a 1D-LSTM or no-temporal-connection baseline, and clarify the training objective (Eq. 3) with a justification or ablation of the summed-KL formulation.

- Report all results over multiple random seeds (at least 3) and include error bars or confidence intervals.

- Add a systematic analysis of failure cases (e.g., occlusions, identical objects, object entry/exit) with qualitative examples and quantitative breakdown.

Without the actual table, these quantitative claims cannot be verified. The reader cannot assess the metrics used, the variance across runs, or whether the improvements are statistically significant. This is a fundamental completeness issue: the results section must include the referenced table. comments.title: Missing Table 2 for generalization claims
comments.quote: jects). Table 2 shows that our F-ARI and MSE scores are at least  $50\%$  better than those for R-NEM, and ARI scores are just marginally worse and only on the binary data. I
comments.explanation: The claim that F-ARI and MSE scores are 'at least 50% better than those for R-NEM' is presented as a quantitative result, but Table 2 is not included in the extracted manuscript. Without the actual table values, the reader cannot verify this claim or assess the magnitude of improvement. The paper must include Table 2 with the reported scores to substantiate this statement. Rewrite the sentence to reference the missing table or include the table in the manuscript.

comments.title: R̂ definition not linked to ELBO summation
comments.quote: thermore, to reduce the overall complexity of the model and to make it easier to train, we set $\widehat{R}:=\max(R-t,1)$ (see Fig. 2 for an illustration).
comments.explanation: The paper defines R̂ = max(R-t,1) but does not explain how this quantity is used in the ELBO. The ELBO in Eq. (3) sums over r=1 to R, not r=1 to R̂. If R̂ is intended to replace R in the summation (e.g., for early frames where t is small, allowing more refinements), this is not stated. Moreover, the claim that this reduces complexity is unclear: if the sum over r still goes to R, the complexity is unchanged. If the sum is truncated to R̂, then the ELBO changes with t, which may introduce a bias. The authors should clarify whether R̂ replaces R in the summation and, if so, justify why the resulting objective remains a valid ELBO.

comments.title: Unsupported runtime complexity claim
comments.quote: The proposed method is expressive enough to model the multimodality of our image formation process and posterior, yet its runtime complexity is smaller than that of its static counterpart.
comments.explanation: The claim that the runtime complexity is 'smaller than that of its static counterpart' is stated without any derivation or reference. The static counterpart (IODINE) processes each frame independently with R refinement steps, giving O(T·R) complexity. The proposed 2D-LSTM processes a 2D grid of size T × R, which naively is O(T·R) as well. The paper later mentions O(R² + T) vs O(R·T), but this is not introduced here. Without a concrete complexity analysis or a citation, the claim is unsubstantiated and potentially misleading. The authors should either provide the complexity comparison in this section or remove the claim until it is properly derived.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about high accuracy without color
comments.quote: Additionally, we show that our model has a high accuracy even without color information.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims that the model 'has a high accuracy even without color information,' but the section text provides no evidence or reference to experiments supporting this claim. The paper-level issues note that quantitative results are missing, and no ablation study on color vs. grayscale input is mentioned. This claim is vague ('high accuracy') and unsupported. The authors should either remove this sentence or cite specific results from the main text that demonstrate this capability.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about prediction of object trajectories
comments.quote: Our method improves the overall quality of decompositions, encodes information about the objects’ dynamics, and can be used to predict trajectories of each object separately.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims the model 'can be used to predict trajectories of each object separately,' but the paper-level issues note that prediction results show only marginal and short-lived improvements (first 3-7 frames) and that no error accumulation analysis is provided. The claim of predicting 'trajectories' implies accurate long-term forecasting, which is not supported by the reported results. The authors should qualify this claim (e.g., 'can predict trajectories for a short horizon') or provide evidence of long-term prediction accuracy.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about R-NEM's limitations
comments.quote: Since R-NEM cannot cope well with colored data or the perspective of scenes, it is only evaluated on the Bouncing Balls dataset (binary), producing high-error results in the first frames, a phenomenon not observed with our model.
comments.explanation: The claim that R-NEM 'cannot cope well with colored data or the perspective of scenes' is presented as a fact without supporting evidence or citation. The paper does not provide a reference demonstrating that R-NEM fails on colored data, nor does it show experimental results on colored data for R-NEM (since it is only evaluated on binary Bouncing Balls). The claim appears to be an inference from the authors' own experimental design, but without a controlled experiment (e.g., testing R-NEM on colored Bouncing Balls or CLEVRER), this statement is speculative. Rewrite the sentence to simply state that R-NEM was evaluated only on binary data due to its design assumptions, and remove the unsupported claim about its inability to handle colored data or perspective.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated attribution of SEQ-IODINE's poor performance
comments.quote: SEQ-IODINE tends to perform even worse than IODINE in many experiments, which we attributed to exploding gradients caused by limited refinement steps and a lack of dynamics modeling.
comments.explanation: The authors attribute SEQ-IODINE's worse performance to 'exploding gradients caused by limited refinement steps and a lack of dynamics modeling,' but no evidence is provided to support this explanation. No gradient norm plots, training loss curves, or ablation studies are presented to confirm that exploding gradients occur. The phrase 'limited refinement steps' is also vague—it is unclear how many refinement steps SEQ-IODINE uses and why that would cause exploding gradients. Rewrite the sentence to simply report the empirical observation that SEQ-IODINE performs worse, and remove the speculative attribution unless supporting evidence is provided.

comments.title: Overclaiming generalization to complex appearance and motion
comments.quote: We presented a novel unsupervised learning framework capable of precise scene decomposition in multi-object videos with complex appearance and motion.
comments.explanation: The claim of handling 'complex appearance and motion' is contradicted later in the same section, where the authors state 'Our model is currently not targeting complex textured datasets.' The quantitative evaluation is entirely on synthetic datasets (Bouncing Balls, CLEVRER) with simple shapes and motions. The only real-world test (Grand Central Station) is qualitative and shows coarse segmentations that do not cleanly separate individual people. The paper does not test on any standard benchmark with complex textures (e.g., DAVIS, YouTube-VOS). Therefore, the opening claim overstates what has been demonstrated. Rewrite the quoted text as 'We presented a novel unsupervised learning framework capable of precise scene decomposition in multi-object videos with simple appearance and motion, and we discuss limitations for complex textured scenes.' because the evidence only supports decomposition of simple synthetic scenes and the authors themselves acknowledge the limitation.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about detecting new objects faster
comments.quote:
