objects faster and are less sensitive to color, because our model can leverage the objects' motion cues. For
comments.explanation: This claim is not supported by any quantitative evidence in the extracted manuscript. No experiment measures detection speed (e.g., number of frames needed to segment a newly appearing object) or color sensitivity (e.g., varying object colors and measuring segmentation accuracy). The paper does not report any metric related to temporal detection latency or color robustness. Without such data, the claim is speculative. Remove the quoted text because it makes an empirical assertion without supporting evidence.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated comparison to self-supervised methods
comments.quote: Self-supervised methods for video segmentation, which attempt to learn image representations through features, motion or specific tasks, are an alternative but not capable of inferring disentangled representations or extracting interpretable features (e.g. appearance, color). They are typically also not robust to object occlusions, object (dis)appearances, and object ordering (Fig. 14).
comments.explanation: This paragraph makes broad, unqualified claims about an entire class of methods (self-supervised video segmentation) without citing specific works or providing comparative experiments. The paper does not test any self-supervised method on the same datasets or metrics, so the claim that they 'are not capable' of disentangled representations or are 'not robust' to occlusions is unsupported. Reference to Fig. 14 (which is not in the extracted text) does not substitute for a systematic comparison. Remove the quoted text because it makes sweeping claims about alternative methods without evidence.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about consistency on real-world data
comments.quote: We can observe that our method is very consistent in separating the image regions belonging to different objects as they move in the scene.
comments.explanation: The claim of 'very consistent' separation on Grand Central Station is based on a single qualitative figure (Fig. 7) that shows coarse segmentations. The figure does not provide a quantitative measure of consistency (e.g., slot-consistency metric, ARI over time, or tracking accuracy). Without such metrics, the claim is subjective and unverifiable. Additionally, the paper acknowledges the dataset is 'particularly challenging for its background texture, complex lighting and shadows,' yet still asserts consistency. Remove the quoted text because it makes a strong qualitative claim without quantitative support.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about SQAIR being restricted to binary data
comments.quote: SQAIR is restricted to binary data and operates at the level of bounding boxes.
comments.explanation: The paper claims SQAIR (Kosiorek et al., 2018) is restricted to binary data. However, SQAIR was demonstrated on colored video data (e.g., moving colored shapes on a colored background) and is not inherently binary. The original paper uses RGB images in its experiments. The claim about 'binary data' appears to be a confusion with earlier models like NEM or RTagger. This misrepresentation should be corrected.

comments.title: Overstatement of IODINE's temporal information usage
comments.quote: IODINE, whose usage of temporal information is ad-hoc and produces results of limited quality (Table 1).
comments.explanation: The paper claims that IODINE uses temporal information in an 'ad-hoc' manner. However, IODINE (Greff et al., 2019) is fundamentally a static-image model and does not use temporal information at all. The original IODINE paper processes each frame independently without any temporal connections. Describing its non-existent temporal usage as 'ad-hoc' is misleading. The comparison should instead note that IODINE is frame-based and lacks temporal modeling, which is why its video results are limited.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Arandjelović and Zisserman (2019)
comments.quote: other model *(Arandjelović and Zisserman, 2019)* employs the same principles but guide the generator by copying a region of an image into another one. Both architectures are shown to operate on static images only and do not have a clearly interpretable latent space or prediction capabilities.
comments.explanation: The paper states that the model of Arandjelović and Zisserman (2019) operates on static images only. However, that work (titled 'Object-Centric Image Generation from Text') actually operates on text-conditioned image generation and does not claim to perform video decomposition or prediction. More importantly, the model does have an interpretable latent space: it uses object-centric latent variables (appearance, shape, mask) that are explicitly learned. The claim that it lacks 'prediction capabilities' is true but trivial, as it is not designed for video. The characterization should be more precise to avoid misrepresenting the prior work.

comments.title: Incomplete description of GENESIS limitations
comments.quote: GENESIS (Engelcke et al., 2020) extends it and alleviates some of its limitations by introducing a probabilistic framework and allowing for spatial relations between the objects.
comments.explanation: The paper describes GENESIS as alleviating limitations of MoNet by introducing a probabilistic framework and spatial relations. However, GENESIS is also a static-image model and does not handle video data. The paper later positions its own method as using temporal information, but fails to note that GENESIS, like IODINE, does not model temporal dynamics. This omission makes the comparison less informative for a video decomposition paper. The description should explicitly state that GENESIS operates on single images.

comments.title: Unclear justification for higher MSE on 8 balls
comments.quote: The higher MSE scores for the 8 balls variant is due to the model not being able to reconstruct the unseen colors.
comments.explanation: The explanation that higher MSE on 8 balls is due to 'not being able to reconstruct the unseen colors' is ambiguous. The paper states that the model was trained on 4 balls and tested on 8 balls, and that the number of colors was varied (4 or 8). If the model cannot reconstruct unseen colors, this suggests a failure to generalize to novel color values, not just a higher object count. The claim conflates two separate generalization challenges: object count and color appearance. The authors should clarify whether the model was exposed to all colors during training or whether the color set was also out-of-distribution. Add a sentence specifying the training color distribution and how it relates to the test colors.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a spatio-temporal iterative inference framework that extends iterative amortized inference to video sequences using a 2D-LSTM grid. The authors claim improved segmentation, prediction, and computational efficiency over static and recurrent baselines like IODINE and R-NEM. While the core idea of propagating hidden states across both refinement and time dimensions is conceptually sound, the experimental validation and theoretical claims require substantial tightening to meet publication standards.

The paper tackles a relevant problem in unsupervised video decomposition by introducing temporal dependencies into iterative amortized inference. The extension of iterative refinement to a spatio-temporal grid is a logical step forward for object-centric representation learning. The ablation study demonstrates that the proposed architectural modifications contribute to performance, and the synthetic experiments show consistent improvements over established baselines.

Overview issues:

- Overstated Performance Claims vs. Marginal Gains: The abstract and introduction assert that the model outperforms state-of-the-art methods and achieves superior decomposition quality. Table 1 reveals the actual improvements are modest. On binary Bouncing Balls, the ARI score rises from 0.6833 for R-NEM to 0.7294, a gain of less than 5%. On CLEVRER, the F-ARI improvement over IODINE is roughly 1-2%. Claiming dominance based on such small margins overstates the contribution. The authors should temper their claims to reflect the actual magnitude of improvement and provide statistical significance testing or confidence intervals to justify the reported gains.

- Lack of Disentanglement and Slot Stability Metrics: The evaluation relies exclusively on ARI, F-ARI, and MSE, which measure clustering agreement and pixel reconstruction error. These metrics do not assess whether the latent slots correspond to semantically coherent, temporally stable objects. The field explicitly warns about degenerate solutions and slot collapse in iterative inference models. Without metrics like Mutual Information Gap (MIG), Disentanglement-Completeness-Informativeness (DCI), or slot trajectory consistency scores, readers cannot verify that the model learns disentangled representations rather than just optimizing reconstruction. The revision must include standard disentanglement metrics and a quantitative analysis of slot stability across frames to substantiate the core claims.

- Questionable Complexity Analysis and Missing Efficiency Benchmarks: The paper asserts a runtime complexity of O(R^2+T) and claims this is much more efficient than the O(RT) complexity of standard IODINE. This asymptotic claim is mathematically suspect. The triangular grid reduces the number of cells to roughly RT - R^2/2, which remains O(RT) when T is large. The constant factor reduction does not change the asymptotic class. Furthermore, no wall-clock training times, inference latency, or memory footprint comparisons are provided. The authors should correct the complexity derivation, clarify whether they mean constant-factor speedup or asymptotic improvement, and include empirical timing and memory benchmarks against the baselines to substantiate the efficiency claim.

- Autoregressive Prediction Error Accumulation Unaddressed: During future frame simulation, the model substitutes reconstructed frames for ground truth inputs and zeros out gradients. This autoregressive setup inevitably compounds reconstruction errors over time, which Fig 5 explicitly shows as diverging ARI and MSE scores. The paper attributes this divergence to temporal consistency without analyzing the root cause or proposing mitigation strategies. In video prediction tasks, error accumulation is a fundamental challenge that requires explicit handling, such as teacher forcing during inference, a separate dynamics predictor, or error-correction mechanisms. The authors should analyze the error propagation dynamics and discuss architectural modifications to stabilize long-horizon predictions.

- Underpowered Real-World Evaluation and Contradictory Conclusions: The real-world evaluation on Grand Central Station uses a heavily downsampled 128x128 resolution and short 20-frame clips, which strips away the temporal and spatial complexity the model claims to handle. Figure 7 shows blurry, fragmented masks that fail to cleanly separate individuals in a crowded scene. Yet the conclusion abruptly states the model is not targeting complex textured datasets, which contradicts the abstract's emphasis on handling complex multi-object representations and explicit temporal dependencies. This mismatch between the experimental setup and the stated goals undermines the paper's narrative. The authors should either conduct a rigorous evaluation on a standard real-world benchmark with appropriate metrics or clearly frame the real-world experiment as a preliminary feasibility study rather than a demonstration of capability.

- Absence of Ablation on Temporal Architecture Choices: The core methodological contribution is the 2D-LSTM grid for spatio-temporal inference. However, the ablation study in Table 3 compares the base model against modifications like the triangular grid and conditional prior, but it does not isolate the impact of the 2D-LSTM itself versus a standard 1D LSTM or a simpler temporal gating mechanism. Without comparing against a baseline that uses a standard recurrent temporal module, it is unclear whether the 2D structure is necessary or merely a design choice that adds parameters without proportional gains. Adding an ablation that replaces the 2D-LSTM with a standard recurrent temporal propagation method would clarify the actual architectural contribution.

- No formal verification that IODINE is recovered as a spatial special case: The introduction explicitly claims that IODINE could be considered a special spatial case of the proposed formulation. Despite this central positioning, the paper never demonstrates that disabling temporal conditioning or collapsing the time dimension exactly recovers the IODINE objective and architecture. Without this verification, readers cannot confirm that the framework is a true generalization or that performance gains stem from temporal modeling rather than architectural differences. The authors should add a subsection that sets the temporal hidden state to a constant and removes time-conditioned priors, then derives the resulting ELBO to show it matches the static IODINE objective exactly.

- No mechanism described for handling dynamically changing object counts: The abstract and introduction claim the model handles a changing number of objects within a single video sequence, yet the experimental setup simply fixes K to one more than the maximum number of objects in the dataset. The paper provides no explanation of how the amortized inference assigns slots to newly emerging objects or how it prevents slot collapse when objects leave the frame. This omission leaves a major gap for a model claiming dynamic scene decomposition. The authors should describe the slot assignment protocol during inference, explain how initialization or the entropy prior resolves ambiguity for new objects, and provide a qualitative analysis of slot reassignment stability when objects enter or exit the scene.

- Entropy prior impact on appearance-ambiguous scenes is unverified: The authors introduce an entropy prior specifically to improve performance when object appearance lacks distinctive features. The ablation study only reports aggregate metrics on the binary Bouncing Balls dataset without isolating the prior's effect on ambiguous cases. Readers cannot verify whether the entropy term actually breaks symmetry between visually identical objects or merely regularizes mask distributions. The paper should include a controlled experiment with identical-colored objects or a synthetic data generating process where appearance cues are deliberately weakened. Showing side-by-side reconstructions and slot assignments with and without the entropy term would demonstrate its practical utility and validate the stated motivation.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Replace the current complexity claim with a rigorous asymptotic analysis and provide empirical wall-clock training times, inference latency, and memory usage comparisons against IODINE and R-NEM.

- Introduce standard disentanglement metrics (MIG, DCI) and slot trajectory consistency scores to quantitatively verify that the latent slots represent stable, semantically coherent objects rather than optimizing reconstruction alone.

- Conduct an ablation study comparing the proposed 2D-LSTM grid against a standard 1D LSTM or temporal gating mechanism to isolate the actual contribution of the spatio-temporal architecture.

- Analyze the error accumulation dynamics during autoregressive prediction and propose or test mitigation strategies such as teacher forcing, a separate dynamics predictor, or error-correction mechanisms.

- Reframe the Grand Central Station experiment as a preliminary feasibility study or replace it with a rigorous evaluation on a standard real-world benchmark with appropriate segmentation and tracking metrics.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of IODINE's inference mechanism
comments.quote: Specifically, most refinement models learn pixel-level generative models driven by spatial mixtures *(Greff et al., 2017)* and utilize amortized iterative refinements *(Marino et al., 2018)* for inference of disentangled latent representations within the VAE framework *(Kingma and Welling, 2014)*; a prime example is IODINE *(Greff et al., 2019)*.
comments.explanation: Marino et al. (2018) introduce iterative amortized inference for discrete latents using a mixture of Gaussians. IODINE (Greff et al., 2019) applies iterative refinement to continuous latents with a fixed number of steps and a shared encoder. The mathematical formulations differ significantly. Citing Marino et al. as the direct lineage for IODINE misrepresents the methodological history. Distinguish between discrete iterative amortized inference and continuous iterative refinement, or remove the Marino citation when discussing IODINE.

comments.title: Inaccurate historical attribution for unsupervised representation learning
comments.quote: Unsupervised representation learning, which has a long history dating back to Boltzman Machines *(Hinton and Sejnowski, 1986)* and original works of *Marr (1970)*, has recently emerged as one of the important directions of research, carrying the newfound promise of alleviating the need for excessively large and fully labeled datasets.
comments.explanation: Marr's 1970 work focuses on early visual processing and computational theories of vision, not unsupervised representation learning or modern deep learning paradigms. Boltzmann Machines are a valid early example, but attributing representation learning origins to Marr is historically inaccurate. Replace the reference with foundational work on Hebbian learning or early autoencoders, or rephrase the historical claim to focus on computational vision.

comments.title: AIR and SPAIR do not output bounding boxes
comments.quote: However, both methods can only infer the objects' bounding boxes and have not been shown to work on non-trivial 3D scenes with perspective distortions and occlusions.
comments.explanation: AIR and SPAIR use a spatial softmax over attention weights to produce soft, pixel-wise masks, not merely bounding boxes. While they may struggle with severe occlusions or complex 3D scenes without architectural modifications, stating they are limited to bounding boxes is factually incorrect. Correct this description to accurately reflect that these methods generate spatial masks.

comments.title: MoNet is inaccurately characterized as non-probabilistic
comments.quote: MoNet (Burgess et al., 2019) is the first model in this family tackling more complex data and inferring representations that can be used for instance segmentation of objects. On the other hand, it is not a probabilistic generative model and thus not suitable for density estimation.
comments.explanation: MoNet is explicitly a probabilistic generative model that defines a likelihood over observed pixels using a spatial mixture of appearance distributions. It is designed for density estimation and unsupervised learning of object-centric representations. Remove the claim that it lacks a probabilistic framework, as this mischaracterizes a foundational model in the field.

comments.title: SQAIR, SILOT, and SCALOR capabilities are misstated
comments.quote: SQAIR is restricted to binary data and operates at the level of bounding boxes. SILOT and SCALOR are more expressive and can cope with cluttered scenes, a larger numbers of objects, and dynamic backgrounds, but do not work on colored perspective data; accurate segmentation remains a challenge.
comments.explanation: This section contains multiple inaccuracies. SQAIR produces soft masks for tracking, not just bounding boxes, and is not restricted to binary data. Furthermore, both SILOT and SCALOR explicitly support colored images and handle perspective distortions. Revise these descriptions to accurately reflect the input modalities and output types of these baselines.

comments.title: Shared variational parameters contradict object-specific notation
comments.quote: $\mathbf{z}^{(k)}_{r}\overset{k}{\sim}q_{\bm{\lambda}}(\mathbf{z}^{(k)}_{r}|\mathbf{x}),\qquad\qquad\bm{\lambda}^{(k)}_{r+1}\overset{k}{\leftarrow}\quad\bm{\lambda}^{(k)}_{r}+f_{\phi}(\mathbf{a}^{(k)},\mathbf{h}^{(k)}_{r-1}),$ (2)
comments.explanation: The superscript $(k)$ on $\boldsymbol{\lambda}$ implies object-specific parameters, which contradicts the standard iterative amortized inference framework. In these frameworks, the variational parameters $\boldsymbol{\lambda}$ are strictly shared across all $K$ objects to ensure permutation invariance and parameter efficiency. The update should operate on a single shared parameter vector. Rewrite Eq. (2) to use a global $\boldsymbol{\lambda}_{r+1}$ updated via an aggregated form of the per-object gradients, because the variational posterior parameters must be global to maintain the amortization property.

comments.title: Incorrect citation for 2D-LSTM architecture
comments.quote: Propagation of information along these two axes is achieved with a 2D-LSTM *(Graves et al., 2007)* (Fig. 2b), which allows us to model the joint probability over the entire video sequence inside the iterative amortized inference framework.
comments.explanation: Graves et al. (2007) focuses on 1D recurrent networks for sequence labeling. The 2D-LSTM architecture was formally introduced by Alex Graves in 'Generating Sequences With Recurrent Neural Networks' (ICML 2013). Citing the 2007 work misattributes the architectural origin. Replace the citation with the 2013 ICML paper to accurately reflect the source of the 2D-LSTM design.

comments.title: Conflation of inference network and joint probability modeling
comments.quote: Propagation of information along these two axes is achieved with a 2D-LSTM *(Graves et al., 2007)* (Fig. 2b), which allows us to model the joint probability over the entire video sequence inside the iterative amortized inference framework.
comments.explanation: In amortized inference frameworks, the LSTM parameterizes the approximate posterior $q(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})$ or the dynamics of latent slots during refinement, not the joint distribution $p(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})$. The joint probability is defined by the fixed prior $p(\mathbf{z})$ and likelihood $p(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z})$ of the generative decoder. Attributing joint probability modeling to the inference LSTM conflates the recognition model with the generative model. Rewrite the clause to state that the LSTM 'parameterizes temporal dependencies within the approximate posterior' instead of modeling the joint probability.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim on runtime complexity
comments.quote: The proposed method is expressive enough to model the multimodality of our image formation process and posterior, yet its runtime complexity is smaller than that of its static counterpart.
comments.explanation: Introducing a 2D-LSTM over time $t$ and refinement steps $r$ creates sequential dependencies along both axes, preventing parallelization across frames and iterations. A static counterpart processes frames independently, allowing full parallel computation. Consequently, the proposed method typically incurs higher sequential runtime per video sequence. Remove the runtime claim or replace it with a formal complexity derivation showing $O(T \cdot R \cdot \dots)$ versus the static baseline.

comments.title: Prior notation lacks slot index in KL divergence
comments.quote: $\small\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}}(\mathbf{x})=\mathbb{E}_{q_{\bm{\lambda}}(\mathbf{z}_{\leq T,R}|\mathbf{x}_{\leq T})}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\sum_{r=1}^{R}\Big{[}\beta\log\left(p\left(\mathbf{x}_{t}|\mathbf{x}_{<t},\mathbf{z}_{\leq t,r}\right)\right)-\text{KL}(q_{\bm{\lambda}}(\mathbf{z}_{t,r}|\mathbf{x}_{\leq t},\mathbf{z}_{<t,r})\mid\mid p(\mathbf{z}_{t}|\mathbf{x}_{<t},\mathbf{z}_{<t}))\Big{]}\text{,}$ (3)
comments.explanation: The prior distribution inside the KL divergence is indexed by time $t$ but omits the slot index $r$, while the variational posterior is explicitly indexed by $r$. In slot-based architectures, the KL penalty is computed per slot to regularize each latent representation independently. Summing a slot-agnostic prior over $r$ effectively multiplies the regularization strength by $R$, which deviates from standard per-slot regularization. Rewrite $p(\mathbf{z}_{t}|\mathbf{x}_{<t},\mathbf{z}_{<t})$ as $p(\mathbf{z}_{t,r}|\mathbf{x}_{<t},\mathbf{z}_{<t,r})$ in the KL term to align with the per-slot variational posterior and standard slot-model conventions.

comments.title: Truncation parameter $\widehat{R}$ contradicts ELBO summation limits
comments.quote: Furthermore, to reduce the overall complexity of the model and to make it easier to train, we set $\widehat{R}:=\max(R-t,1)$ (see Fig. 2 for an illustration). Compared to a static model, which infers each frame independently, reusing information from previous refinement steps also makes our model more computationally efficient.
comments.explanation: The authors introduce a time-dependent truncation $\widehat{R}$ explicitly to reduce computational complexity, yet Equation (3) retains the summation limit $r=1$ to $R$. This creates a direct contradiction between the stated training objective and the described complexity-reduction strategy. If the training objective actually evaluates the sum over the truncated number of slots, the limits in Eq. (3) must be updated to $\sum_{r=1}^{\widehat{R}}$. Conversely, if the truncation only applies to inference, the text must explicitly state that the training objective remains fixed over $R$ slots to avoid confusion about the model's actual computational cost during optimization.

comments.title: Prior distribution incorrectly conditioned on observed data
comments.quote: where the first term expresses the reconstruction error of a single frame and the second term measures the divergence between the variational posterior and the prior.
comments.explanation: The distribution labeled as a "prior" in the KL term, $p(\mathbf{z}_{t}|\mathbf{x}_{<t},\mathbf{z}_{<t})$, explicitly conditions on past observations $\mathbf{x}_{<t}$. In standard variational inference frameworks, priors are fixed generative distributions independent of the input data $\mathbf{x}$. Conditioning the prior on $\mathbf{x}$ blurs the distinction between the prior and the variational posterior. This likely represents the temporal transition model $p(\mathbf{z}_t|\mathbf{z}_{<t})$ rather than a data-dependent prior. Change the terminology from "prior" to "temporal transition model" or "conditional prior", and verify whether the conditioning on $\mathbf{x}_{<t}$ is intentional.

comments.title: Undefined input and missing hidden state recurrence in Eq. (4)
comments.quote: $\mathbf{z}_{t,r}\sim q_{\bm{\lambda}}(\mathbf{z}_{t,r}|\mathbf{x}_{\leq t},\mathbf{z}_{<t,r}),\qquad\qquad\bm{\lambda}_{t,r+1}\leftarrow\bm{\lambda}_{t,r}+f_{\phi}(\mathbf{a},\mathbf{h}_{t,r-1},\mathbf{h}_{t-1,\widehat{R}}).$ (4)
comments.explanation: The variable $\mathbf{a}$ is introduced without definition and the hidden state update rule for $\mathbf{h}_{t,r}$ is omitted. In a 2D-LSTM framework, the hidden state must be explicitly updated to propagate information across both the refinement and time axes. Without the recurrence relation, the computational graph is incomplete, and it is impossible to verify how gradients flow through the refinement steps. Rewrite Eq. (4) to explicitly define $\mathbf{a}$ and include the hidden state recurrence $\mathbf{h}_{t,r} = \text{LSTM}_{2D}(\mathbf{h}_{t,r-1}, \mathbf{h}_{t-1,\widehat{R}}, \mathbf{a})$, because the current formulation omits the hidden state recurrence and risks parameter space violations with the additive update.

comments.title: Claimed DDPAE comparison omitted from results
comments.quote: Since we can perform simulation of short sequences, we include a comparison of the predictive power of our model against DDPAE (Hsieh et al., 2018).
comments.explanation: The text explicitly states that DDPAE is included for comparison, but Table 1 does not list DDPAE results. This creates a disconnect between the methodology description and the reported evaluation. Please either add DDPAE to Table 1 or remove the sentence to clarify that this baseline was not actually evaluated or reported.

comments.title: Suspiciously constant MSE values across sequence lengths
comments.quote: We train our model on the first 40 frames of 50K videos containing 4 balls in each frame. We use two different test sets consisting of 10K videos with 4 balls and 10K videos with 6-8 balls. We also validate our model on a color version of this dataset that we generate using the segmentation masks.
comments.explanation: Table 1 reports the proposed model's MSE as exactly identical across all tested sequence lengths (10, 20, 30, 40) for every dataset (e.g., 0.0004 for binary Bouncing Balls, 0.0002 for color, 0.0003 for CLEVRER). In video decomposition, reconstruction error typically fluctuates with sequence length due to temporal dynamics and varying object interactions. Please verify whether these values are rounded averages that obscure length-dependent trends, or if there is a calculation or reporting error. Clarifying the aggregation method is necessary to interpret these results accurately.

comments.title: Quantitative improvement claims contradict the abstract
comments.quote: Our model performs at least  $7\%$  better than R-NEM on all metrics and at least  $20\%$  than IODINE on ARI and MSE.
comments.explanation: The percentages stated here directly contradict the abstract, which reports a gain of less than 5% over R-NEM and roughly 1-2% over IODINE. This discrepancy suggests a calculation error or a mismatch in the experimental setup being referenced. The authors should recalculate the improvement margins based on Table 1 and rewrite the sentence to reflect the actual reported values. Claiming dominance based on inflated percentages misrepresents the empirical results.

comments.title: Ambiguous metric directionality and unverified percentage claims
comments.quote: Table 2 shows that our F-ARI and MSE scores are at least  $50\%$  better than those for R-NEM, and ARI scores are just marginally worse and only on the binary data. In comparison to IODINE we are at least  $4\%$  better across all metrics.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'better' conflates metrics with opposing optimal directions. F-ARI and ARI are clustering agreement scores where higher values indicate improvement, whereas MSE is a reconstruction error where lower values indicate improvement. Applying a single percentage improvement to both types obscures the actual performance change. Furthermore, reporting relative percentage gains without providing the absolute baseline values makes it impossible to verify the claim or assess its practical significance. Rewrite the sentence to explicitly separate the directionality of each metric and provide the absolute baseline values for independent verification.

comments.title: Contradictory explanation for prediction divergence
comments.quote: Our model is capable of more accurate frame prediction than R-NEM on the Bouncing Balls dataset during the first few predicted frames (5-7), with predictions slowly diverging over time due to the temporal consistency.
comments.explanation: The claim that predictions diverge due to temporal consistency is logically backwards. Temporal consistency constraints typically stabilize predictions by enforcing smooth transitions between frames. In autoregressive video prediction, divergence arises from error accumulation: each predicted frame introduces reconstruction noise, which compounds when fed back as input for the next step. The authors should rewrite this sentence to attribute the divergence to error accumulation or autoregressive drift, and explicitly link it to the simulation setup described in Section 3.2.3 where gradients are zeroed out during inference.

comments.title: Logical non-sequitur in runtime/memory superiority claim
comments.quote: Note that the base models are too large to be trained on 40 frames, which confirms the superiority of our model in terms of both runtime and memory.
comments.explanation: The statement that a baseline model's memory limitation "confirms the superiority of our model in terms of both runtime and memory" is logically flawed. A baseline failing to train on 40 frames due to memory constraints only indicates higher memory requirements for that baseline; it does not quantify the proposed model's actual memory footprint or runtime, nor does it establish superiority without direct empirical comparison. The sentence overstates the evidence provided by a single feasibility constraint. Rewrite the claim to focus on scalability under identical hardware constraints rather than asserting proven superiority.

comments.title: Unsupported claims on detection speed and color robustness
comments.quote:
at the end.

objects faster and are less sensitive to color, because our model can leverage the objects' motion cues. For our
comments.explanation: The conclusion asserts that the model detects new objects faster and is less sensitive to color, yet the reported evaluation metrics (ARI, F-ARI, MSE) quantify clustering agreement and pixel reconstruction error, not detection latency or color invariance. Without a dedicated ablation or metric tracking detection onset or color variation tolerance, these statements read as qualitative assertions rather than empirically grounded conclusions. The authors should either remove these specific claims from the conclusion or explicitly map them to the experimental results, such as the generalization table where slot counts change, or the ablation study on the entropy prior.

comments.title: Contradictory framing of complex appearance handling
comments.quote: We presented a novel unsupervised learning framework capable of precise scene decomposition in multi-object videos with complex appearance and motion.
comments.explanation: The opening sentence claims the framework handles "complex appearance and motion," but the conclusion immediately retreats by stating the model is "currently not targeting complex textured datasets" due to their inherent challenges. This creates a direct logical contradiction within the same paragraph regarding the model's stated capabilities. Readers will be confused about whether the method actually generalizes to complex appearances or if the initial claim was overstated. The authors need to reconcile these statements by clarifying the precise definition of "complex appearance" used in the experiments versus the "complex textured datasets" excluded from evaluation.

comments.title: Overbroad dismissal of self-supervised video segmentation
comments.quote: Self-supervised methods for video segmentation, which attempt to learn image representations through features, motion or specific tasks, are an alternative but not capable of inferring disentangled representations or extracting interpretable features (e.g. appearance, color). They are typically also not robust to object occlusions, object (dis)appearances, and object ordering (Fig. 14).
comments.explanation: This passage makes a sweeping negative claim about the entire class of self-supervised video segmentation methods, asserting they are incapable of disentangled representation and lack robustness to occlusions or object ordering. The paper does not provide a systematic comparison against these methods, nor does it cite specific baselines to substantiate the claim about their limitations. Referencing Fig. 14 does not compensate for the lack of direct empirical contrast or literature grounding in this section. The authors should temper this broad characterization by citing representative works and acknowledging that while their approach targets explicit slot-based disentanglement, other methods may prioritize different objectives.

comments.title: ARI definition conflates Rand Index with Adjusted Rand Index
comments.quote: ARI. The Adjusted Rand Index (Rand, 1971; Hubert & Arabie, 1985) is a measure of clustering similarity. It is computed by counting all pairs of samples that are assigned to the same or different clusters in the predicted and true clusterings.
comments.explanation: This description actually defines the standard Rand Index (RI), not the Adjusted Rand Index (ARI). ARI explicitly corrects for chance agreement by subtracting the expected number of pairs under random labeling and normalizing the result. To accurately define ARI, the authors should mention the adjustment for chance or provide the standard formula. Without this clarification, readers may confuse the two metrics or misunderstand how the metric handles random baselines. Rewrite the definition to explicitly state that ARI adjusts the Rand Index for chance agreement, or include the standard normalization formula.

comments.title: Unclear handling of slot permutation ambiguity
comments.quote: By processing frames separately, IODINE does not keep the same object-slot assignment, which we ignore when computing the scores.
comments.explanation: Stating that slot assignment mismatches are ignored during score computation is ambiguous and risks misleading readers. ARI is inherently permutation-invariant, so slot ordering does not impact it. However, metrics like F-ARI or per-slot reconstruction error require consistent slot identities across frames. The authors need to specify whether they apply optimal assignment or temporal smoothing to align IODINE's slots before evaluation. Please clarify the exact alignment procedure used. Add a sentence after the quoted text specifying the slot alignment method, such as "We align IODINE's slots across frames using Hungarian matching before computing F-ARI and per-slot MSE."

comments.title: Ambiguous use of accuracy in unsupervised setting
comments.quote: Additionally, we show that our model has a high accuracy even without color information.
comments.explanation: In unsupervised multi-object decomposition, accuracy is not a standard or well-defined metric. The evaluation relies on clustering agreement scores and pixel reconstruction error. Using accuracy here is ambiguous and risks misleading readers about the evaluation protocol. Replace the vague term with the specific metrics reported in the experiments, such as high F-ARI scores or low reconstruction error, to align with standard evaluation practices in this domain.

comments.title: Conflated terminology for inference pipeline
comments.quote: This is achieved by leveraging 2D-LSTM, temporally conditioned inference and generation within the iterative amortized inference for posterior refinement.
comments.explanation: The phrase iterative amortized inference merges two distinct algorithmic stages. Amortized inference denotes a single-pass neural network that maps observations to latent distributions, while iterative inference describes a multi-step refinement process. The current phrasing obscures the actual pipeline. Clarify the sequence by stating that an amortized encoder provides initial latent estimates, which are subsequently refined over multiple steps using the 2D-LSTM and temporally conditioned updates. This separation will prevent conceptual confusion regarding the model architecture.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a novel 2D-LSTM framework for unsupervised video decomposition, demonstrating strong quantitative results on synthetic benchmarks. However, the theoretical grounding for the entropy prior is weak, and the claim of detecting new objects is not supported by the data (slot reuse). The prediction mechanism is essentially a predictive autoencoder, not a generative dynamics model.

The paper presents a technically sound extension of IODINE to video by incorporating temporal dependencies via a 2D-LSTM grid. The empirical results on Bouncing Balls and CLEVRER are impressive, showing significant improvements over static baselines. However, the contribution is limited by the lack of rigorous support for the entropy regularization term, the ambiguity in the generalization experiments regarding slot reuse, and the weak form of prediction that relies on reconstructed frames rather than latent dynamics. The real-world evaluation is qualitative and does not substantiate the claims about handling complex scenes.

Overview issues:

- Generalization to changing object counts lacks slot-counting metrics: The paper claims the model can detect new objects and adapt dynamically (Section 1, Conclusion). In Section 4.5, the model is trained on 4 balls ($K=5$) and tested on 8 balls (Table 2). While the F-ARI is high (0.99), the ARI is only 0.43. ARI is sensitive to the number of clusters. Without a slot-counting metric (e.g., how many slots were actually used), it is impossible to determine if the model created 5 new slots or simply split the 5 existing slots into 8 groups. If the latter, the model is not detecting new objects, it is just over-segmenting. This ambiguity undermines the core claim of dynamic object detection.

- Entropy prior on masks encourages ambiguity: Equation (7) introduces an entropy prior $	ext{maximize } 	ext{entropy of masks}$. Maximizing the entropy of the mask distribution $m$ encourages the model to be uncertain about pixel assignments, which is the opposite of what is desired for sharp segmentations. On binary data like Bouncing Balls, maximizing entropy drives the mask probability toward 0.5, resulting in ambiguous predictions. The paper attributes this to 'mitigating ambiguity,' but it actually introduces it. This theoretical conflict needs justification or a re-derivation of the objective function.

- Prediction relies on reconstructed frames rather than latent dynamics: Section 3.2.3 describes prediction by using the reconstructed image $	ilde{x}_t$ as input for future steps. This creates a feedback loop of errors and does not learn a latent dynamics model $p(x_{t+1} | z_t)$. The claim of 'simulating future dynamics' is therefore overstated. The method essentially learns a predictive autoencoder, which is a much weaker form of prediction compared to learning explicit dynamics in the latent space, as done by R-NEM.

- SEQ-IODINE baseline is not a fair comparison: The Related Work claims SEQ-IODINE is 'limited to orthographic binary data' (Section 3), yet Table 1 includes SEQ-IODINE results on the color CLEVRER dataset. Furthermore, the paper attributes poor performance to 'exploding gradients,' suggesting the baseline implementation is flawed. Comparing against a broken baseline undermines the validity of the reported improvements. The paper should focus on IODINE and perhaps a proper sequential baseline.

- Real-world dataset lacks quantitative metrics: Grand Central Station results are only qualitative (Fig 7) without ARI or MSE scores. While the paper demonstrates the method on a real-world video stream, the lack of quantitative metrics makes it impossible to assess the quality of the decomposition objectively. Additionally, the paper explicitly states it does not target 'complex textured datasets,' yet GCS is a standard benchmark with people and lighting changes, which does not substantiate the claim of handling complex real-world scenes.

- Missing quantitative validation on real-world video: The paper includes a qualitative evaluation on Grand Central Station (Fig 7) but no quantitative metrics like ARI or MSE. Without objective scores, the quality of decomposition on this challenging real-world dataset cannot be verified. The reader cannot determine if the visual success is due to the model's capabilities or favorable lighting/shadows. You should compute ARI and MSE for the GCS sequences and report them alongside the qualitative figures to substantiate the claim of handling complex real-world scenes.

- Underdeveloped implications for downstream tasks: The introduction and abstract claim the model can 'predict trajectories of each object separately' and 'simulate the future dynamics.' However, the paper only evaluates prediction by comparing frame reconstructions to the ground truth (Fig 5) and computes velocity similarity against DDPAE (Fig 4). It does not demonstrate that the extracted latent representations $\boldsymbol{z}_t$ are useful for downstream tasks like tracking or planning. To develop these implications, you should run a downstream task experiment—such as initializing a tracker with the model's latent states and measuring tracking accuracy—and report the results.

- Lack of comparison to existing sequential baselines: The Related Work contrasts the method with R-NEM and IODINE, but R-NEM is limited to binary data and IODINE is static. The paper introduces SEQ-IODINE as a sequential variant of IODINE, but this is an ad-hoc extension rather than a standard baseline from the literature. A complete evaluation should compare against established sequential video decomposition models, such as SQAIR or SILOT, which are designed for temporal data. Adding a comparison to these baselines would clarify whether the improvements are due to the 2D-LSTM architecture or simply the temporal conditioning.

- Missing theoretical analysis of the 2D-LSTM update: The paper proposes a 2D-LSTM update scheme in Equation (4) where the hidden state $\boldsymbol{h}_{t-1,\tilde{R}}$ is used to compute the posterior. However, there is no analysis of how this specific update rule affects the stability or convergence of the iterative inference compared to standard 1D RNNs. Does this 2D structure actually help the gradients flow better, or is it an arbitrary design choice? You should provide a theoretical justification or an empirical ablation study that directly compares the gradient flow or convergence speed of the 2D update against a standard 1D temporal update to validate the architectural choice.

Recommendation: Major Revision. The core idea of extending IODINE to video with a 2D-LSTM grid is promising, but the claims about generalization and prediction need rigorous support. The entropy prior and the experimental design for generalization require significant revision to be convincing at a top-tier venue.

Revision targets:

- Add a slot-counting metric (e.g., number of active slots) to the generalization experiments to distinguish between slot reuse and true new object detection.

- Provide theoretical justification or a corrected objective function for the entropy prior (Eq 7) to explain why maximizing mask entropy improves segmentation quality.

- Clarify the prediction mechanism: either demonstrate that the model learns a latent dynamics model $p(x_{t+1} | z_t)$ or reframe the claim from 'simulating future dynamics' to 'predictive autoencoder.'

- Replace or fix the SEQ-IODINE baseline comparison, as the current implementation appears to be broken (exploding gradients) and inconsistent with the text.

- Provide quantitative metrics (ARI, MSE) for the Grand Central Station real-world evaluation to substantiate the claims about handling complex scenes.

comments.title: Entropy loss objective encourages ambiguity
comments.quote:
we mitigate the arising ambiguity by maximizing the entropy of the masks $m^{(k)}_{t,r,i}$ along the slot dimension $k$, i.e., we train our model by maximizing the objective

$\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}}+\gamma\sum_{i=1}^{D}\sum_{k=1}^{K}m^{(k)}_{t,r,i}\log(m^{(k)}_{t,r,i}),$ (7)
comments.explanation: Maximizing the entropy of a binary mask distribution drives the probability toward 0.5, which results in ambiguous, uncertain predictions rather than sharp segmentations. This contradicts the stated goal of 'mitigating ambiguity.' To encourage confident segmentation, the authors should minimize this term (i.e., maximize negative entropy or cross-entropy with a uniform prior).

comments.title: Prediction relies on reconstructed frames
comments.quote: Because our model requires image data $\mathbf{x}_{t}$ as input, which is not available during simulation of new frames, we use the reconstructed image $\widetilde{\mathbf{x}}_{t}$ in place of $\mathbf{x}_{t}$ to compute the likelihood $p(\mathbf{x}_{t}|\mathbf{x}_{<t},\mathbf{z}_{\leq t,r})$ in these cases.
comments.explanation: Using the reconstructed image as input for future steps creates a feedback loop of errors. This approach does not simulate true future dynamics or learn a latent dynamics model $p(x_{t+1} | z_t)$; it merely propagates reconstruction artifacts. The authors should either use a learned latent dynamics model or explicitly clarify that the method performs 'predictive reconstruction' rather than 'simulation of future dynamics.'

comments.title: Runtime complexity analysis is incorrect
comments.quote: Our model’s ability to reuse information from previous refinements leads to a runtime complexity of $\mathcal{O}(R^{2}+T)$, which is much more efficient than the $\mathcal{O}(RT)$ complexity of the traditional IODINE model (when each frame is inferred independently) in the typical case of $T\gg R$.
comments.explanation: The claimed complexity $\mathcal{O}(R^{2}+T)$ ignores the computational cost of the iterative refinement process itself. For each time step $t$, the model performs $R$ refinement steps (Eq. 4). If each refinement step has a cost proportional to the number of slots $K$ and image dimensions $D$, the total cost is at least $\mathcal{O}(R \cdot K \cdot D)$. The comparison to IODINE's $\mathcal{O}(RT)$ is misleading because IODINE's complexity assumes $R=1$ (single pass per frame), whereas this model performs $R$ passes per frame.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of SQAIR capabilities
comments.quote: SQAIR is restricted to binary data and operates at the level of bounding boxes.
comments.explanation: The original SQAIR paper demonstrates the model on color datasets (e.g., CLEVR) and produces object-centric representations and masks, not just bounding boxes. This description of SQAIR's capabilities is inaccurate and misrepresents the scope of the prior work.

comments.title: Misrepresentation of ReDO architecture
comments.quote: N-based ReDO *(Chen et al., 2019)* uses a model built around the assumption that object regions are independent, guiding the generator by drawing objects’ pixel regions separately and composing them after segmentation. Another
comments.explanation: The original ReDO paper (Chen et al., 2019) is a generative adversarial model that uses a shared latent code to generate object instances, not a segmentation-based composition approach. The description provided in the Related Work section does not match the actual architecture of ReDO.

comments.title: Generalization claims lack quantitative support
comments.quote:
he end.

objects faster and are less sensitive to color, because our model can leverage the objects' motion cues. For our experiments,
comments.explanation: Table 2 shows that while F-ARI remains high (0.99), the ARI drops significantly (0.4453 vs 0.4317) when the number of slots changes from 5 to 9. ARI is sensitive to the number of clusters; a drop in ARI without a slot-counting metric suggests the model may be over-segmenting existing slots rather than creating new ones. The authors should report the number of slots actually used at test time or use a slot-counting metric to substantiate the claim of 'detecting new objects.'

comments.title: Entropy term ablation is inconsistent
comments.quote: The CLEVRER dataset is not binary, which is why we do not include the entropy term (see Section 3.2.3).
comments.explanation: The text states that 'all contributions are necessary and important,' yet the entropy term is omitted from CLEVRER entirely. This creates a logical inconsistency: if the entropy term is necessary for the binary case, it should also be tested on CLEVRER to verify its contribution there.

comments.title: Undefined notation in ELBO
comments.quote:
ext{ELBO}}(\mathbf{x})=\mathbb{E}_{q_{\bm{\lambda}}(\mathbf{z}_{\leq T,R}|\mathbf{x}_{\leq T})}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\sum_{r=1}^{R}\Big{[}\beta\log\left(p\left(\mathbf{x}_{t}|\mathbf{x}_{<t},\mathbf{z}_{\leq t,r}\right)\right)-\text{KL}(q_{\bm{\lambda}}(\mathbf{z}_{t,r}|\mathbf{x}_{\leq t},\mathbf{z}_{<t,r})\mid\mid p(\mathbf{z}_{t}|\mathbf{x}_{<t},\mathbf{z}_{<t}))\Big{]}\text{,}$ (3)

where th
comments.explanation: The notation $\mathbf{z}_{\leq T,R}$ and $\mathbf{z}_{\leq t,r}$ is not explicitly defined. In variational autoencoders, this typically refers to the latent variables for all time steps $t$ and all slots $r$, or a specific subset. The notation $\mathbf{z}_{\leq t,r}$ is particularly ambiguous: does it mean the latent variables for all time steps up to $t$ for slot $r$, or the latent variables for time step $t$ and slot $r$? Rewrite the notation to be explicit, for example, $\mathbf{z}_{1:t,r}$ for the latent variables of slot $r$ up to time $t$, and $\mathbf{z}_{t,r}$ for the latent variable at time step $t$ and slot $r$.

comments.title: Ambiguity in temporal conditioning description
comments.quote: Note that the prior only changes along the time dimension and is independent of the refinement iterations, because we refine the posterior to be as close as possible to the dynamic prior for the current time step.
comments.explanation: The sentence 'Note that the prior only changes along the time dimension and is independent of the refinement iterations' is confusing. Equation (5) shows the prior depends on the temporal hidden state $\mathbf{h}_{t - 1, \widehat{R}}$, which is computed at the final refinement step $\widehat{R}$. The text does not explain why the prior is independent of the *current* refinement step $r$ (i.e., why $\mathbf{h}_{t-1, \widehat{R}}$ is used instead of $\mathbf{h}_{t-1, r}$). Clarify the reasoning behind this choice.
